title: Solar Installers: June's Pipeline Surge and the Working Capital It Consumes
linkedin:
June is the strongest sales month for most solar installation businesses. The STC incentive window and EOFY consumer spending combine to fill the order book weeks ahead of time.

What gets less attention is what that surge does to cash.

The STC rebate process takes days to two weeks to complete. When a large volume of jobs finishes within the same four to six-week window, those STC receipts can land in July, after the financial year has closed. Add superannuation contributions due by 28 July for the April to June quarter, and the cash position in early July can feel considerably tighter than the June revenue figures suggest it should.

The other pressure worth naming is unit economics. Most solar installation businesses track revenue per job and total jobs per month. Fewer consistently calculate a real contribution margin per job category: residential systems, commercial rooftop, battery-only additions, EV charging.

Panel and inverter costs have moved considerably in recent years. An installer pricing today against older cost assumptions while paying current materials costs will find the bank account telling a quieter story than the revenue line.

The EOFY period, when a full year of job data is available, is the natural moment to review which categories are genuinely profitable at current costs and which have drifted.

For businesses thinking about eventual sale, the June 30 financials are the foundation from which a valuation is built. Owner-operated businesses where the principal personally holds key subcontractor relationships tend to attract lower multiples than businesses where those functions sit within the team.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness
facebook:
If your solar installation business has had a strong June, the cash position in early July can still feel tight. STC receipts for June jobs often land two weeks later, right alongside Q4 superannuation contributions due 28 July.

Are you tracking where that cash actually lands, or just watching the revenue figure?

The latest ProfitPulse post covers the STC timing gap, unit economics per job type, and what to review before the new financial year gets moving.

google:
June is typically the strongest month of the year for solar installation businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria. The STC incentive deadline and EOFY consumer spending combine to drive quote volumes and committed installation pipelines well ahead of schedule.

What the strong revenue figures can obscure is the cash timing that comes with that volume. STC rebate payments take days to two weeks to process, meaning a high volume of June completions often sees receipts land in July. Combined with superannuation contributions due by 28 July for the April to June quarter, the early July cash position often feels considerably tighter than June's performance suggests it should.

There is also the unit economics question. Panel and inverter costs have moved materially in recent years. Solar installation businesses that have not reviewed their contribution margin per job category, across residential systems, commercial rooftop, battery-only additions, and EV charging, may be working with thinner margins than their historical pricing model implied.

The weeks after June 30, when a full year of financial and job data is available, are the natural moment to run that review. For businesses also thinking about growth or eventual sale, the EOFY financials are the starting point for any working capital or valuation conversation.

ProfitPulse works with trade and installation businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria at this point in the calendar. Read the full post on the ProfitPulse blog:

https://profit-pulse.com.au/solar-installers-june-s-pipeline-surge-and-the-working-capital-it-consumes
